title: Agents, Actions, and Environments
concept: Every reinforcement learning setup breaks down into three parts: the agent that decides, the environment it acts within, and the actions it's allowed to take.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is a reinforcement learning agent trying to maximize?

options.text: Its total reward over time
Its reward on the very next step only
The number of actions it takes

exercises.explain: Right! The agent aims for the best total reward across the whole task, not just the next payoff.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A reinforcement learning agent sometimes gives up a small reward now for a bigger reward later.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: True! That trade-off is exactly how agents maximize total reward over time.

cards.title: Ordering at a Pizza Shop
cards.body: Picture a pizza shop. You (the customer) are standing in front of a menu, deciding what to order. The shop, the menu, the other customers in line — that's the world around you. "Order a pepperoni slice" is one of the choices you could make. Every one of those pieces has a name in reinforcement learning.

cards.title: The agent: the one making decisions
cards.body: The agent is whatever is doing the deciding — a game character, a delivery robot, a thermostat controller. Its whole job is to look at its situation and pick an action.

cards.title: The environment: everything outside the agent
cards.body: The environment is the world the agent acts inside — everything it doesn't fully control. For a video-game character, the environment is the level layout, enemies, and physics. For a thermostat, it's the room, the weather outside, and the people opening doors.

cards.title: The action: a move the agent is allowed to make
cards.body: An action is one specific choice available to the agent at a given moment. A self-driving car's agent might choose from actions like accelerate, brake, or turn left. A chess-playing agent's actions are the legal moves on the board.

cards.title: The environment isn't the agent's enemy
cards.body: It's tempting to think of the environment as something working against the agent. Really, the environment is just neutral — it's the setting where actions play out and rewards get handed out. A calm lake and a hostile enemy are both just "environment."

right.text:
Reacts to the action
and hands back a reward

exercises.prompt: In a maze-solving robot, what counts as the environment?

options.text: The maze itself — the walls, paths, and dead ends
The robot's decision-making program
The move the robot just made

exercises.explain: Right! The maze is the world the robot operates in — that's the environment.

exercises.prompt: True or false: An action is the same thing as the agent.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: False! The agent is the decision-maker; an action is just one of the choices it's allowed to make.

exercises.sentence: The ___ is the decision-maker that chooses an action inside an environment.
exercises.explain: The agent is the one doing the deciding — everything else in the setup responds to its actions.

exercises.prompt: In a game where a character can move up, down, left, or right around a grid, which of these is an action?

options.text: Move right
The 10x10 grid layout
The character earning 5 points

exercises.explain: Right! "Move right" is one specific choice the agent can make — that's an action.

exercises.prompt: A thermostat-controlling agent can choose to raise the temperature, lower it, or leave it unchanged. What role does the room's actual temperature and the people walking in and out play?

options.text: They're part of the environment the agent is reacting to
They're additional agents
They're actions the thermostat can take
They don't matter to reinforcement learning at all

exercises.explain: Exactly! The room, its temperature, and the people in it make up the environment surrounding the thermostat agent.

exercises.prompt: A vacuum-cleaning robot decides whether to keep cleaning a room or return to its charging dock. The room's furniture layout and how much dust is on the floor change over time. Match each part to its role: the robot, the furniture and dust levels, and "return to dock."

options.text: Robot = agent, furniture/dust = environment, "return to dock" = action
Robot = environment, furniture/dust = agent, "return to dock" = reward
Robot = action, furniture/dust = agent, "return to dock" = environment
All three are the same thing: the agent

exercises.explain: Exactly! The robot decides (agent), the room and its state form the environment, and "return to dock" is one action it can choose.

exercises.prompt: A stock-trading program can choose to buy, sell, or hold a stock each day, based on the current market conditions. Why is the market itself considered the environment rather than the agent?

options.text: Because the market isn't the one making trading decisions — it just reacts to and surrounds the agent's actions
Because the market always loses money
Because the market is one of the trading program's actions
Because only humans can be part of an environment

exercises.explain: Exactly! The market is the world the trading agent operates within — it responds to actions but isn't the decision-maker itself. Next up: how rewards and penalties actually shape an agent's behavior.
